title: How humorous is AI? Exploring ChatGPT’s role in humor generation and human-AI interaction
research_questions: Can GPT-4o produce humor that rivals human creativity across sentence-based and image-based humor generation tasks?
How do positive versus negative social contexts influence the funniness, effectiveness, and humor-style distribution of GPT-4o and human humor responses?
Which of Martin et al.’s four humor styles is considered most effective for coping with negative social contexts by humans and GPT-4o?
Can GPT-4o assistance help people generate humorous responses that manage interpersonal conflict more effectively?

datasets_used.name: Study 1 image humor task adapted from Yam et al. (2019)
Study 1 sentence humor task adapted from Nusbaum et al. (2017)
Study 1 human and GPT-4o humor generation responses
datasets_used.size: 100 human participants and 100 AI responses simulated by ChatGPT; two image-task items and two sentence-task items per participant

datasets_used.name: Study 2 positive and negative contexts adapted from Cao et al. (2024)
Study 2 human and GPT-4o humor coping responses
datasets_used.size: 97 human participants and 97 AI responses simulated by ChatGPT; two contexts per participant

datasets_used.name: Martin et al. (2003) four humor types material
Study 3 GPT-4o humor-type response set
datasets_used.size: 100 humor responses for each type of humor; 10 humor responses from each humor type, creating 40 humor responses in total for subsequent evaluation

datasets_used.name: Study 4 workplace conflict humorous response set
datasets_used.size: 20 GPT-4o-generated humorous responses; 200 participant final responses; 60 randomly selected responses from Part 1, including 20 responses by human participants with AI assistance, 20 generated solely by human participants, and 20 generated by AI

methods_proposed.name: Four-study experimental evaluation of GPT-4o humor generation and interaction
methods_proposed.summary: The paper uses four preregistered experiments to compare GPT-4o and humans on humor generation, humor coping, humor-style evaluation, and AI-assisted conflict-response generation. The design combines blind human ratings, expert humor-style coding, GPT-4o-as-rater comparisons, and sender/recipient evaluations in a workplace-conflict scenario.

humor_theories.detail: The paper uses Martin et al.’s four humor types—affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive, and self-defeating—to classify Study 2 responses and to generate and evaluate Study 3 stimuli.

Study 2 motivates the expectation that GPT-4o will generate safe, socially appropriate forms of humor, especially affiliative and self-enhancing humor, by linking model safety objectives to benign violation theory.
The paper operationalizes humor coping as a subset of humor generation used to cope with stress in daily life, drawing on Martin and Lefcourt (1983).

evaluation_methods.detail: Study 1: 112 American university students, blind to the study, rated captions or sentences on a 7-point funniness scale; each caption or sentence was rated by 14 raters.
Study 2: 104 American university students, blind to the study, rated funniness on a 7-point scale; each humor response was rated by 13 raters.
Study 2: two psychology experts specializing in humor research categorized responses according to Martin et al.’s four humor types.
Study 2: 40 American university students, blind to the study, rated effectiveness of negative-context responses on a 7-point scale; each response was rated by 10 raters.
Study 3: 400 human participants rated GPT-4o-generated responses for funniness, likability, and effectiveness on 7-point scales.
Study 3: GPT-4o rated the same humor responses, with each rating conducted in a separate chat and 400 new chat sessions total.
Study 4 Part 1: humor senders self-rated their submitted responses for funniness, likability, effectiveness, and relief.
Study 4 Part 2: 350 human participants evaluated selected responses from the recipient perspective for funniness, likability, effectiveness, and relief.
Study 4 Part 2: GPT-4o-simulated evaluators rated the same selected responses from the recipient perspective.

human_samples.population: 40 men, 57 women, 3 other genders; M_age = 24.65 years, SD_age = 3.66; 59 White, 26 Black or African American, 6 Asian, 3 Hispanic or Latino, 3 Native American or Indigenous, 3 Other
human_samples.observations: Each participant completed two image-task items and two sentence-task items.
Each rater rated 100 captions or sentences; each caption or sentence was rated by 14 raters.

human_samples.population: 48 men, 47 women, 2 other genders; M_age = 24.04 years, SD_age = 3.74; 63 White, 21 Black or African American, 6 Asian, 4 Hispanic or Latino, 3 Other
human_samples.observations: Each participant responded humorously to one positive and one negative context.
Each rater rated about 48 humor responses; each humor response was rated by 13 raters.

human_samples.population: psychology experts specializing in humor research
human_samples.observations: They categorized human and GPT-4o responses using the four humor types proposed by Martin et al. (2003).
Each rater rated about 48 negative-context humor responses; each humor response was rated by 10 raters.

human_samples.population: 184 men, 210 women, 6 other genders; M_age = 40.05 years, SD_age = 13.38
human_samples.geography: U.S. sample described in the abstract
human_samples.observations: Each rater evaluated 10 humor responses; each type of humor response got 100 ratings.
human_samples.detail: Study 3 human raters of funniness, likability, and effectiveness.

human_samples.population: 69 men, 127 women, 4 other genders; M_age = 40.28 years, SD_age = 11.84; 140 White, 29 Black or African American, 16 Asian, 11 Hispanic or Latino, 1 Native American or Indigenous, 3 Other
human_samples.geography: U.S. sample described in the abstract
human_samples.observations: Participants generated humorous workplace-conflict responses; 107 were assigned to the AI condition and 93 to the control condition.

human_samples.population: 171 men, 176 women, 3 other genders; M_age = 40.54 years, SD_age = 13.62; 202 White, 105 Black or African American, 20 Asian, 18 Hispanic or Latino, 1 Native American or Indigenous, 4 Other
human_samples.geography: U.S. sample described in the abstract
human_samples.observations: Human participants evaluated 60 selected responses from the recipient perspective; each response was evaluated by 50 humans.

judges.name: 112 American university students
judges.detail: Study 1 funniness ratings of human and GPT-4o image captions and sentence completions.

judges.name: 104 American university students
judges.detail: Study 2 funniness ratings of positive- and negative-context humor responses.

judges.name: two psychology experts specializing in humor research
judges.detail: Study 2 categorization of responses into affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive, self-defeating, and other humor.

judges.name: 40 American university students
judges.detail: Study 2 effectiveness ratings for negative-context humor responses.

judges.name: 400 human participants
judges.detail: Study 3 ratings of GPT-4o-generated humor-type responses for funniness, likability, and effectiveness.

judges.name: GPT-4o
judges.detail: Study 3 GPT-4o ratings of the same humor-type responses in separate chats.

judges.name: Study 4 humor senders
judges.detail: Study 4 Part 1 self-ratings of submitted responses for funniness, likability, effectiveness, and relief.

judges.name: 350 human participants
judges.detail: Study 4 Part 2 recipient-perspective ratings of AI, AI-assisted, and human-generated responses.

generation_selection.n: 100 simulated human responses; two image-task items and two sentence-task items
generation_selection.detail: GPT-4o was instructed to simulate 100 human responses based on the statistical summary of the human participants’ demographic information; no candidate selection procedure was reported.

generation_selection.scope: Study 2 GPT-4o simulated humor coping generators
generation_selection.detail: GPT-4o was instructed to simulate 97 human participants based on demographic summaries; no candidate selection procedure was reported.

generation_selection.n: 100 humor responses for each type of humor; 10 randomly selected from each humor type
generation_selection.detail: This was multi-candidate generation followed by random, not quality-based, selection for evaluation.
Participants in the AI condition were presented with one randomly selected response from the pool.

generation_selection.n: one AI suggestion shown per participant in the AI condition
generation_selection.detail: Participants could integrate their original response with the AI suggestion, replace their response with the AI response, or keep their original response.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: The paper states that it used OpenAI’s ChatGPT (GPT-4o model, August 2024 version) via the ChatGPT web platform.

inference_budgets.scope: Studies 1 and 2 GPT-4o simulated participant generation
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports the number of simulated GPT-4o participants, 100 in Study 1 and 97 in Study 2, but not the number of chats/calls, token limits, or sampling settings.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Each rating was conducted in a separate chat; GPT-4o rated each type of humor response 100 times, requiring a total of 400 new chat sessions.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 100 humor responses for each type of humor were generated; 10 were randomly selected from each humor type.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report temperature, top-p, seeds, token limits, or cost.

inference_budgets.scope: Study 4 GPT-4o suggestions and simulated evaluators
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Each response was evaluated by 50 GPT-4o-simulated evaluators.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 20 GPT-4o-generated humorous responses formed the suggestion pool.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report the number of GPT-4o chats/calls used to create the pool or simulated evaluators, nor sampling settings or token budgets.

human_baselines.scope: Study 1 image and sentence humor generation
human_baselines.description: 100 human participants completed the same two image and two sentence humor tasks as GPT-4o-simulated participants, and outputs were rated blind by human raters.
human_baselines.detail: Human and GPT-4o responses were evaluated on the same 7-point funniness scale.

human_baselines.scope: Study 2 humor coping in positive and negative contexts
human_baselines.description: 97 human participants and 97 GPT-4o-simulated participants responded to the same positive and negative contexts.
human_baselines.detail: Human and GPT-4o outputs were rated for funniness, negative-context effectiveness, and coded for humor type.

human_baselines.description: 400 human participants and GPT-4o rated the same GPT-4o-generated humor responses for funniness, likability, and effectiveness.
human_baselines.detail: This is a human-judgment baseline for GPT-4o-as-rater, not a human-generation baseline.

human_baselines.scope: Study 4 workplace-conflict humor generation and assistance
human_baselines.description: Human-generated control responses, GPT-4o-assisted human responses, and GPT-4o-generated responses to the same conflict scenario were evaluated.
human_baselines.detail: Part 1 compared sender self-ratings in AI versus control conditions; Part 2 compared recipient-perspective ratings across AI, AI-assisted, and human-generated responses.

mitigations: Human responses were newly collected in the studies.
Human raters in Studies 1 and 2 were blind to the study.
Studies were preregistered and prompts/materials were provided in supplementary materials.
contamination_risk.basis: Several stimuli and materials were adapted from or based on existing public research, including Yam et al. (2019), Nusbaum et al. (2017), Cao et al. (2024), and Martin et al. (2003), and no decontamination test is reported. The main comparisons rely on newly generated human and GPT-4o outputs rated by humans, which lowers but does not eliminate risk.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Claims about GPT-4o’s own ratings in Study 3, convergence between human and GPT-4o evaluations of humor types, and GPT-4o-simulated recipient ratings in Study 4 depend on LLM-as-judge results.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: The major generation claims in Studies 1, 2, and 4 are supported by human raters or human sender/recipient ratings; Study 3 reports parallel human ratings of the same responses.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLM judging is not the sole basis for the paper’s main claim that GPT-4o outperforms humans on text-based humor and negative-context coping, but it is part of the evidence for AI-human agreement and recipient-perspective evaluation.

reporting_gaps: The exact GPT-4o model snapshot or API identifier is not reported beyond “August 2024 version.”
Sampling parameters such as temperature, top-p, seeds, and decoding settings are not reported.
Token limits, number of calls/chats for Studies 1 and 2, and monetary cost are not reported.
Some prompts, operational instructions, scenarios, and visual stimuli are only referenced as supplementary materials rather than shown in the main text.
Study 4 Part 2 reports overall patterns and F-thresholds in the text, but exact cell means are not provided in the main text.
Recruitment platform and detailed geography are not specified in the Study 3 and Study 4 method sections, although the abstract describes the sample as racially diverse and from the U.S.

headline_results.finding: In Study 1, humans were funnier on the image task, but GPT-4o was funnier on the sentence task, with a significant group-by-task interaction.
headline_results.value: interaction F(1, 198) = 90.33, p < .001, ηp² = .31; image task human M = 3.88, se = .06 vs GPT-4o M = 3.24, se = .06; sentence task GPT-4o M = 4.03, se = .06 vs human M = 3.57, se = .06
headline_results.dataset: Study 1 human and GPT-4o humor generation responses
headline_results.best_system: GPT-4o on sentence task; humans on image task

headline_results.finding: In Study 2, GPT-4o responses were rated funnier than human responses in both positive and negative contexts, with a larger advantage in the negative context.
headline_results.value: main effect of group F(1, 192) = 61.32, p < .001, ηp² = .24; interaction F(1, 192) = 8.10, p = .005, ηp² = .04; positive context GPT-4o M = 4.08, se = .08 vs human M = 3.60, se = .09; negative context GPT-4o M = 4.21, se = .08 vs human M = 3.27, se = .08
headline_results.dataset: Study 2 human and GPT-4o humor coping responses

headline_results.finding: In Study 2, GPT-4o negative-context humor was rated more effective than human negative-context humor.
In Study 2, GPT-4o overwhelmingly produced self-enhancing humor in the negative context, while humans produced all four humor types.
headline_results.value: negative context self-enhancing humor: GPT-4o 96/97 and 97/97 across the two experts; human 56/58; human responses also included affiliative 17/19, aggressive 16/13, self-defeating 7/4, and other 0/3
headline_results.dataset: Study 2 human and GPT-4o humor coping responses

headline_results.finding: In Study 3, human raters rated self-enhancing humor as the funniest, most likable, and most effective response to the negative context.
headline_results.value: self-enhancing humor human ratings: funny M=4.91, se=.09; likable M=5.06, se=.09; effective M=5.37, se=.08; aggressive humor was lowest: funny M=3.17, se=.10; likable M=3.41, se=.10; effective M=3.56, se=.10

headline_results.finding: In Study 4 Part 1, participants with AI assistance rated their final conflict responses higher than control participants on all four sender-side outcomes.
headline_results.value: condition effects Fs(1, 198) > 8.79, ps ≤ .003, ηp²s > .04; AI condition: funniness M = 4.28, se = .14, likability M = 4.83, se = .16, effectiveness M = 4.75, se = .15, relief M = 4.67, se = .15; control: M_funniness = 3.54, se = .15, M_likability = 4.02, se = .17, M_effectiveness = 4.01, se = .16, M_relief = 4.02, se = .16
headline_results.dataset: Study 4 workplace conflict humorous response set

key_findings: GPT-4o outperformed humans on text-based sentence completion humor but underperformed humans on image-caption humor.
GPT-4o generated funnier humor coping responses than humans in both positive and negative contexts, with its largest advantage in negative contexts.
GPT-4o’s negative-context humor was rated more effective than human negative-context humor and was coded almost entirely as self-enhancing humor.
Both human raters and GPT-4o treated self-enhancing humor as the best strategy for negative contexts, although GPT-4o also rated self-defeating humor favorably for funniness and likability.

In the workplace-conflict study, AI assistance improved sender self-ratings, but recipient-perspective analyses found pure AI responses rated above AI-assisted responses, which were rated above human responses.
GPT-4o raters generally gave higher ratings than human raters in Studies 3 and 4.
limitations: The research focuses on GPT-4o, and the authors state that findings may not generalize to other AI systems, platforms, or newer models such as GPT-5.
The paper focuses mainly on humor generation in negative contexts and calls for work in other emotional settings such as joy, positive bonding, neutral situations, and ambiguous situations.
Age was not closely examined even though the studies involved different age groups.
The authors note possible cultural bias because humor is culturally rooted and GPT-4o may reflect North American and English-language training data.
The humor content was limited to textual or image-based material and did not include audio or video cues such as tone of voice, facial expression, and gesture.
Study 4 suggests AI-assisted humor underperformed pure AI-generated humor, possibly because of authenticity trade-offs, cognitive load, or friction in integrating human and AI styles.
safety_ethics_notes: All participants received informed consent, and the research received ethical approval from Peking University. The discussion notes potential psychological and social concerns, including feelings of threat or inadequacy from AI superiority and possible over-reliance on AI for support.

artifacts.other: Supplementary data: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chbr.2025.100807; preregistrations: https://aspredicted.org/384d-fnk3.pdf, https://aspredicted.org/47z4-rhzm.pdf, https://aspredicted.org/th5y-t2qn.pdf, https://aspredicted.org/y237-33mv.pdf
